text.title: Message Broker Implementations Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules for mq-broker-implementations. Each rule pairs binary check + sourced rationale. Last rule is the terminal skip-this-methodology guard.

rule.statement: Pick the broker matching the dominant constraint: throughput → Kafka (out of scope); AWS-native → SQS; Python workflows → Celery; existing Redis → Streams; complex routing → RabbitMQ.
rule.rationale: Each broker has a sweet spot; mismatch produces operational pain (retry storms, message loss, unbounded latency).

rule.statement: Every published message MUST go through publisher confirms (RabbitMQ) / acks=all (Kafka) / SendMessage response (SQS) / Celery apply_async result check. Treat fire-and-forget as a bug.
rule.rationale: Without confirms, 'published' means 'in the TCP send buffer'; broker crashes drop the message silently.

rule.statement: Consumer MUST ack the broker AFTER committing the side-effect to the database, not before. Pre-ack creates silent message loss on DB failure.
rule.rationale: Acks before commit hand the broker permission to delete the message; the consumer then crashes and the side-effect is lost.

rule.statement: Every queue MUST have a DLQ wired before going live; max_retries reached → message goes to DLQ, alert fires.
rule.rationale: Without a DLQ, poison messages either block the queue forever or are dropped silently.

rule.statement: Every message body MUST carry a stable idempotency_key field, not rely on broker-assigned message IDs.
rule.rationale: Broker-assigned IDs change on retry; only payload-embedded keys deduplicate reliably.

rule.statement: If the preconditions in `Applies If` fail or any of the `Skip If` conditions is true, return `skipped` and apply no further rules.
rule.rationale: Methodologies are not universal; running them outside their fit-window wastes effort and produces wrong outputs.
